rubric_items.reasoning: The agent correctly diagnosed that the certs Secret had been deleted entirely (not just expiring) and performed a genuine root-cause rotation: generated new node and client.root cert/key pairs signed by the existing CA using the same SAN config, rebuilt the full 5-key Secret, and rolled the StatefulSet so all 3 pods remounted fresh certs from stable storage (the Secret), not just live in-pod state. This is durable — it would survive a pod restart or re-check, since the source of truth (the Secret) was actually updated. Verification confirms SQL works, fingerprint changed, and validity is exactly ~365 days. Minor uncertainty remains since the log's diagnostic head is truncated, but nothing in the visible evidence suggests shortcuts or a papered-over fix.

The visible portion of the log shows a coherent, non-panicked sequence: roll the StatefulSet, correctly interpret transient gossip/liveness timeout noise during pod restart as normal startup churn rather than a TLS failure, wait for rollout to complete, then run all three verification commands in one batch. This reflects sound diagnostic judgment (recognizing benign vs. real errors) rather than trial-and-error. Slight deduction because the log's head (initial cert-generation and secret-recreation steps) is truncated, so the full diagnosis-to-action path isn't fully visible to confirm there was no backtracking earlier.

Tooling matches the task closely: openssl commands run inside the provided openssl-toolbox pod to generate certs signed by the existing CA (as instructed), a targeted secret recreation with exactly the required keys, and a standard `kubectl rollout restart` to get pods to pick up the new Secret. The K8s activity confirms this was lean — only 2 mutations out of 68 total calls, with the rest being reads/verification. No broad deletes, force flags, or scope creep beyond the cockroachdb namespace.

The change was minimal and focused: only 2 mutating operations (Secret recreation + StatefulSet rollout restart) were needed to rotate certs across a 3-node cluster, with no unrelated resources touched. This is close to the smallest viable change for a full cluster-wide cert rotation that must propagate to all pods.
